did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (Convolutional Neural Networks: AlexNet, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, ResNet 50) for feature extraction and machine learning classifiers (e.g., SVM, WNN, QSVM, MNN, ESD) for skin cancer classification. It also employed the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for feature reduction and the RAPS method for model selection, using the ISIC 2017 dataset to classify basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, pigmented benign keratosis, and vascular lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and benchmarking of 51 skin cancer classification models using CNNs (AlexNet, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, ResNet 50), Grey Wolf Optimizer for feature reduction, and machine learning classifiers on the ISIC 2017 dataset. It includes a methodology, results with specific performance metrics (e.g., 94.5% accuracy), and a discussion, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin cancer classification based on an optimized convolutional neural network and multicriteria decision‑making
brief_summary: This study developed and benchmarked 51 skin cancer classification models to identify the optimum paradigm for automated diagnosis. The researchers utilized four Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, and ResNet 50) for feature extraction from the ISIC 2017 dataset, which included basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, pigmented benign keratosis, and vascular lesions. Feature reduction was performed using the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) in two variants, followed by classification with six different machine learning algorithms. To select the best model among the many alternatives, the authors employed the multicriteria decision-making method known as Ranking the Alternatives by Perimeter Similarity (RAPS). The results indicated that the AlexNet algorithm combined with classical GWO yielded the optimum model, achieving a classification accuracy of 94.5%. The study concludes that feature reduction not only reduces training time but also improves classification accuracy, validating the robustness of the RAPS method for model selection.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete skin cancer classes: basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, pigmented benign keratosis, and vascular lesions.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer images using the ISIC 2017 dataset. The study developed 51 models combining CNNs (AlexNet, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, ResNet 50) for feature extraction, Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for feature reduction, and machine learning classifiers (SVM variants, WNN, MNN, ESD) for class prediction. The primary task is assigning each image to one of the four diagnostic categories, evaluated by metrics such as accuracy (94.5%), sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 release, containing dermoscopic images of various skin conditions. The study utilized a refined subset of this dataset, excluding classes with limited representation (actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma).

use.training: 70% of the dataset was used for training the CNN feature extractors and ML classifiers.
use.validation: 15% of the dataset was used for validation during model development.
use.testing: 15% of the dataset was used for testing and evaluating the classification models.

statistical_methods_used.method: Ranking the Alternatives by Perimeter Similarity (RAPS)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To select the optimum model among 51 developed models by ranking them based on multiple evaluation metrics.
To determine the weights of the evaluation criteria (metrics) used in the RAPS method.
To normalize the evaluation metrics for beneficial and nonbeneficial criteria before applying RAPS.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model selection was performed using the multicriteria decision-making method Ranking the Alternatives by Perimeter Similarity (RAPS) combined with the CRITIC method for weighting criteria, rather than statistical hypothesis testing.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Four Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) used for feature extraction: AlexNet, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, and ResNet 50. These were combined with six Machine Learning (ML) classifiers: Cubic Support Vector Machine (CSVM), Wide Neural Network (WNN), Quadratic Support Vector Machine (QSVM), Medium Neural Network (MNN), Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM), and Ensemble Subspace Discriminant (ESD). Feature reduction was performed using two variants of the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO): classical GWO (GWO1) and modified GWO (GWO2).
architecture_used.pretraining: The CNN models (AlexNet, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, ResNet 50) were used as pre-trained feature extractors. The paper does not specify the exact pretraining datasets (e.g., ImageNet) but implies standard pre-trained versions were utilized.

input_size.MobileNet V2: Not explicitly stated (standard MobileNet V2 input is typically 224x224x3, but not reported in text)
input_size.ResNet 50: Not explicitly stated (standard ResNet 50 input is typically 224x224x3, but not reported in text)

Inception V3.components: Small filter sizes (1 × 5, 1 × 7), small convolution layer (1 × 1) before large filters, batch normalization, soft-max layer

ResNet 50.layers: 49 convolutional layers and one FC layer

MobileNet V2.components: Depthwise and pointwise convolution layers, linear bottlenecks, shortcut connections

training_details.learning_rate: Initial learning rate 0.0001, piecewise learning rate schedule
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7, NVIDIA GeForce MX 130, Windows 11, 64 bits, 16 GB RAM

classification_or_output_head.best_performing_classifier: Wide Neural Network (WNN) when combined with AlexNet and GWO1
classification_or_output_head.output_classes: 4 classes: Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Pigmented benign keratosis, Vascular lesions

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset partitioning: 70% for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing.
Dimension adjustment: Images were resized/adjusted to align with the input size requirements of each employed deep learning model (AlexNet: 227x227x3, Inception V3: 229x229x3).

No additional augmentation or image processing: The paper explicitly states that the dataset quality was commendable, rendering augmentation or additional image processing enhancement strategies unnecessary.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper explicitly states that data augmentation was not used because the dataset quality was considered commendable, rendering augmentation or additional image processing enhancement strategies unnecessary.

conclusions: The study developed and benchmarked 51 skin cancer classification models using combinations of four CNNs (AlexNet, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, ResNet 50), two Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) variants, and six machine learning classifiers to identify the optimum paradigm.
The AlexNet algorithm combined with classical GWO (GWO1) and the Wide Neural Network (WNN) classifier yielded the optimum model, achieving a classification accuracy of 94.5%.
Feature reduction using the Grey Wolf Optimizer not only reduces training time but also improves classification accuracy compared to using original features.
The multicriteria decision-making method Ranking the Alternatives by Perimeter Similarity (RAPS) proved robust and effective for selecting the best model among the many alternatives developed.
This work presents the first study to benchmark skin cancer classification with a significant number of models using different configurations in a single study.
The automated diagnosis of skin cancer was improved through the proposed methodology, which has implications for earlier detection and patient healing.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small dataset of only 1,466 images from the ISIC 2017 release, with five clinically significant classes (actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma) excluded due to limited representation, potentially introducing selection bias.

No data augmentation was employed; the authors justified this by stating the dataset quality was 'commendable,' which may limit the model's robustness to variations in real-world dermoscopic images (e.g., hair, blood vessels, noise).

The study lacks external validation, relying solely on a single internal partition (70% training, 15% validation, 15% testing) of one dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between the 51 models; model selection relied on the multicriteria decision-making method RAPS.

The best-performing model achieved an accuracy of 94.5%, which, while high, may not reflect performance on imbalanced or more complex real-world clinical datasets where melanoma detection is critical.

The study benchmarks 51 models but does not compare them against state-of-the-art end-to-end deep learning architectures or recent transformer-based models, limiting the context of its 'optimum' claim.

Computational resources were modest (Intel Core i7, NVIDIA GeForce MX 130), which may not reflect the scalability or deployment feasibility of the proposed optimized pipeline in resource-constrained clinical settings.
